coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal route to graphene quantum dots: Effects of precursor and temperature
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Jian-De
Lai, Gui-Wen
Huq, Mohammad Mahmudul
coredata.dc:description: This work adopts a hydrothermal approach to produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from different precursors including carbon nanotube (CNT), graphene oxide (GO), and carbon black (Super P, SP). The report also explores the effects of hydrothermal temperature on the particle size and photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs. The experiments show that GQDs obtained from CNTs not only have the lowest particle size (~1.56nm) but also offer the highest PL intensity, as compared to the GQDs prepared from GO and SP. The study on temperature effect reveals that GQD size decreases as hydrothermal temperature increases, which can be attributed to the formation of OH radicals that cleaves graphite at higher temperature. The PL study confirms that the GQDs show a blue-shift behavior as the particle size decreases. The as-prepared GQDs produce yellow and blue photoluminescence. Therefore, this study provides an efficient method to tune the PL characteristics of GQDs, easing the development of optoelectronic materials for energy and biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Photoluminescence
Hydrothermal cutting
Carbon nanotubes
Carbon black

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM images of different GQDs and their size-distribution histograms: (a) CNT, (b) CP, and (c) GO precursors.
(a) FTIR and (b) Raman spectra of different GQDs.
UV–visible spectra of different GQDs: (a) CNT, (b) SP, and (c) GO precursors.
Photographs for different GQDs prepared from CNT, SP, and GO precursors (from left to right) under (a) visible light and (b) UV irradiation at 340nm. (c) PL spectra of different GQDs.
AFM images of different GQDs and their size-distribution histograms. The GQDs were prepared by using the hydrothermal cutting technique at (a) 90, (b) 105, (c) 120, and (d) 150°C.
The variation of GQD average particle size with hydrothermal temperature.
UV–visible spectra of different GQDs. The GQDs were prepared by using the hydrothermal cutting technique at (a) 90, (b) 105, (c) 120, and (d) 150°C.

Photographs for different GQDs prepared by using the hydrothermal cutting technique at 90, 105, 120, and 150°C (from left to right) under (a) visible light and (b) UV irradiation at 340nm. (c) PL spectra of different GQDs.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal route to graphene quantum dots: Effects of precursor and temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, Institute of Material Preparation and Applied Technology, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, Fujian, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications
Institute of Material Preparation and Applied Technology
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan 32003, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work adopts a hydrothermal approach to produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from different precursors including carbon nanotube (CNT), graphene oxide (GO), and carbon black (Super P, SP). The report also explores the effects of hydrothermal temperature on the particle size and photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs. The experiments show that GQDs obtained from CNTs not only have the lowest particle size (~1.56nm) but also offer the highest PL intensity, as compared to the GQDs prepared from GO and SP. The study on temperature effect reveals that GQD size decreases as hydrothermal temperature increases, which can be attributed to the formation of OH radicals that cleaves graphite at higher temperature. The PL study confirms that the GQDs show a blue-shift behavior as the particle size decreases. The as-prepared GQDs produce yellow and blue photoluminescence. Therefore, this study provides an efficient method to tune the PL characteristics of GQDs, easing the development of optoelectronic materials for energy and biological applications.

The hydrothermal cutting method at 90–150°C was adopted to adjust average particle size of GQDs, inducing different PL responses and intensities.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs), nanoscale size semiconductor particles, have been emerging as an interesting material due to their eccentric properties such as tunable band gaps, arising from quantum confinement that enables widening of band gap as the size of the material shrink, implying that a range of these materials with different band gaps can be obtained by changing their sizes [1–3]. One implication of this characteristic behavior is that each of these dots, although are essentially the same materials, will radiate lights of specific wavelengths upon excitation by electricity or irradiation according to its size. The QDs have prospective applications in a range of fields including biosensing and bioimaging, photovoltaic devices, light emitting devices, photocatalysis etc. [4–11]. However, conventional semiconductor-based QDs mostly comprise heavy metals, e.g., CdSe, CdS, CdTe, PbSe, PbS, InAs, InP and CdHgTe, severely limiting their applicability because of their high level of toxicity [12–17]. Therefore, many researchers, nowadays, are inclined to graphene quantum dots (GQDs), single or multilayer graphene sheets of sizes generally <20nm [18]. Graphene, a single atomic layer of graphite sheet, is basically a zero band-gap material with infinite exciton Bohr radius [18]. Modification of the size and surface chemistry of graphene enables the adjustment of its band gap. According to density functional theory calculations, GQDs comprising 20 aromatic rings and benzene show band gaps of approximately 2 and 7eV, respectively [19].

Like other nanomaterials, there are, broadly, two approaches to synthesize GDQs: bottom-up and top-down methods. The former involves the synthesis process from aromatic compounds, whereas the later includes the chemical cutting of graphene into GQDs. Hydrothermal method is a promising top-down technique to synthesize GQDs from a variety of precursors, i.e., conductive carbon black (e.g., type: Super P, SP), carbon nanotube (CNT), graphene oxide (GO), and carbon fibers [20–24]. As to conventionally hydrothermal methods, strong oxidizing agents like HNO3, H2SO4 or H2O2 are used to cut carbon nanoparticles into GQDs [25]. In this work, we adopt a hydrothermal method with hydrogen peroxide as a reagent to synthesize GQDs from three different precursors namely CNT, GO and SP, and study their effects on the properties of the final products. The thermal condition of the hydrothermal process is an important parameter in tuning the photoluminescence (PL) properties of GQDs. Therefore, we report the effect of hydrothermal temperature on the PL of products while downsizing graphite flakes into GQDs.

In the present work, the GO sheets were prepared by using a modified Hummers' method involving chemical exfoliation of graphite powder by strong oxidation agents. The SP powders were purchased from Taiwan Maxwave Co. Ltd. The CNTs used in this work were grown by chemical vapor deposition method, using Ni oxide and ethylene as metallic catalyst and carbon source, respectively.

To prepare GQDs, first, 5mg of carbon precursors (i.e., GO, SP, and CNT) was sonicated in 15ml ethanol for 2h. Next, 15ml of H2O2 was added to the suspension. The hydrothermal cutting process was carried out in a 150ml Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. As to study the precursors, the suspension was heated at 120°C for 2h. The product was separated from the liquid using a centrifuge at 12,000rpm. For clarity, the GQD samples were designated as C-GQD, S-GQD and G-GQD, according to their sources: CNT, SP and GO, respectively. In order to study the effects of temperature, the same procedure was followed, except that the autoclave temperature was maintained at four different temperatures: 90, 105, 120 and 150°C.

The size information and topographic image of GQDs were taken by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Ardic P100). Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR, Nicolet Avatar 360 FTIR) spectroscopy was utilized to study the surface functional groups of the as prepared GQDs. 32 scans in the spectral range of 500–2000cm−1 with the resolution of 4cm−1 were performed for each sample. The crystalline nature of the GQDs was analyzed by Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw Micro-Raman spectrometer). An ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (Agilent Technology Cary 60) was employed to obtain the UV–vis absorbance spectra of GQD suspensions with a weight ratio 0.05mg GN to 1mL deionized water in quartz cell. The scan rate of the spectrometer was set at 60nm/min. Distilled water was used as the blank sample. An optic spectrometer (FLS920P Edinburgh Analytical Instrument), equipped with Xe lamp, was used to garner the emission and excitation spectra of GQDs at the excitation wavelength of 340nm. Ethanol was used as a solvent for the suspension during PL characterization.

Fig. 1 (a), (b) and (c) present the AFM images and their particle size distributions of the GQDs prepared from CNT, SP and GO, respectively. All three samples show fairly normal distribution. As can be seen in the AFM images (see the inset of Fig. 1), C-GQD sample shows a significantly lower number of white dots, suggesting lesser presence of GQDs with >5 graphene layers, as compared to other two samples. The average particle sizes of GQD samples follow the order: C-GQD (1.56nm)<G-GQD (2.30nm)<S-GQD (2.88nm). The FTIR spectra of the GQD samples presented in Fig. 2(a) show bands at 1080 and 1640cm−1, assigned to the COC (epoxy) and CO (carbonyl) groups attached to the GQD surface. It is evident from band intensities that C-GQD has the highest density of epoxy and carbonyl groups. Raman spectroscopy is a powerful non-destructive tool to analyze the graphitic structure of carbon materials. Raman spectra of the GQD samples, presented in Fig. 2(b), shows the G band at ~1580cm−1, ascribing to the stacking of hexagonal graphite network plane; and the D band at ~1350cm−1, corresponding to the amorphous carbon or deformation vibrations of a hexagonal ring [26–28]. Basically, the ratio of intensities of G to D band (I D/I G) signifies the amount of defects and surface oxides present on the graphitic surface. As evident from the Raman spectra, I D/I G values of the samples can be ordered as C-GQD (1.14)>G-GQD (0.82)>S-GQD (0.64), clearly indicating that C-GQD possesses the highest amount of surface defects as well as surface oxides, complementing the FTIR study. Overall, the Raman analysis portray that the GQDs graphitic in nature with no impurity phase. Fig. 3 shows UV–visible spectra of GQD samples in distilled water, in which the maximal absorbance can be attributed to π→π* transition in the poly-aromatic system (i.e., CC bond) of graphene samples; and the absorption edge mainly originates from n→π* transitions of nonbonding electrons of adatoms [29–31].

The images of various GQD suspensions under visible light and UV irradiation at 340nm are shown in Fig. 4(a) and (b), respectively. The figure clearly indicates that C-GQD shows a vivid blue color under UV irradiation, whereas both G-GQD and S-GQD samples display dark blue. As to PL spectra, all three samples show the highest PL-excitation intensity at around 420nm. However, the PL intensities were of order: C-GQD>G-GQD>S-GQD, with intensity for C-GQD is approximately 1.3 and 1.5 times as high as G-GQD and S-GQD, respectively. The PL mechanism of GQDs is still not very well understood. The emission property of GQD materials is often attributed to the recombination of electrons and holes, resulting in an emission of photons. This is also referred as the radiative recombination of excitons or electron-hole pairs arising from absorption of photons. Although the PL behavior of GQDs is a size-dependent phenomenon, other factors like presence of defects, shape and surface chemistry are found to be influential. Dangling bonds and the surface defects in GQDs could cause charge carriers to be trapped, suppressing radiative recombination, potentially quenching the emission. However, the higher PL intensity of C-GQD as compared to the others could be well explained by the fact that, with higher density of surface oxide groups, as evident from FTIR and Raman spectroscopy analysis, C-GQD forms a better and more stable dispersion in water.

In order to study the temperature effects on hydrothermal synthesis of GQD, graphite flakes were downsized hydrothermally at four different temperatures: 90, 105, 120 and 150°C. The AFM images and the particle size distributions of the GQD samples are presented in Fig. 5 . Evidently, there is a negative correlation between average GQD particle size and hydrothermal temperature, e.g., from 2.75nm (90°C) to 1.58nm (150°C), as shown in Fig. 6 (the error bars represent one standard deviation of uncertainty). A mechanism is proposed by R. Tian et al. in order to explain the cutting of GO by H2O2 [25]. According to the mechanism, H2O2 dissociates into ·OH at high temperature and then thermally cut graphite sheets into fragments as the hydrothermal reaction carries on. The experimental results, basically, show that the disintegration of graphite is higher at increased temperature.

The UV–vis absorbance spectra data of graphite derived GQDs show similar absorbance characteristics as CNT, GO and SP derived ones, as shown in Fig. 7 . Fig. 8(a) and (b) show the GQD suspensions prepared at different temperatures under visible light and UV irradiation at 340nm, respectively. The GQD samples prepared at 90 and 105°C emit yellow luminescence, whereas the other samples prepared at higher temperature (i.e., 120 and 150°C) emit blue. In addition, the PL spectra presented in Fig. 8(c) show that the highest PL excitation intensity occur at 550, 540, 440 and 430nm for GQD samples prepared at 90, 105, 120 and 150°C, respectively. It is widely known that the excitation wavelength shifts to the blue region as the band gap of QDs increases. Basically, the GQDs are small fragments of graphene in which the effects of quantum size take over. As a result, all GQDs possess non-zero band gaps, which can be modified by tuning the particle size and the surface chemistry. The experimental results obtained here clearly reveal a correlation between the particle size and PL properties of GQDs, which is also supported by previous literatures [32,33]. However, it should be noted that several other properties including surface heterogeneity, defect density, porosity and oxidation level, also play crucial roles in determining the PL properties. Since the hydrothermal cutting was adopted to treat different carbon precursor, the factors are capable of offering a synergistic effect that incorporate with the quantum size, leading to unique PL response. The PL intensity of the GQDs prepared at different temperatures follows an order: 90<105<120<150°C. Few literatures actually address the problem of PL intensity variation. Discussed earlier in Section 3.1, both defect sites within GQDs and quantum size are to suppress radiative recombination of excitons, leading to a fall in PL intensity. Therefore, the rise of PL intensity with hydrothermal temperature could be attributed to the elimination of defect sites in GQDs. Herein, we have emphasized the importance of particle size on the PL behavior of GQDs. On the basis of experimental results, this article reports a simple hydrothermal cutting method to prepare GQDs from several nanocarbon precursors and studied their PL properties. The effects of hydrothermal temperature on the size and PL properties of the hydrothermally exfoliated GQDs from graphite flake have also been discussed.

In summary, the GQDs have been prepared hydrothermally from three different precursors e.g., CNTs, GO and SP. The GQDs synthesized from CNTs were found to have the smallest particle sizes, as compared to GQDs prepared from GO and SP. The PL characterization results confirmed that C-GQDs show the highest PL intensity, which could be attributed to their small sizes. In order to study the effects of temperature on hydrothermal synthesis of GQDs, the graphite flakes have been downsized hydrothermally at four different temperatures, e.g., 90, 105, 120 and 150°C. There has been a negative correlation between the particle size of GQDs and the hydrothermal temperature. The PL experiments revealed that the GQDs show a blue-shift behavior as their sizes shrink, enabling the synthesis of GQDs with different color emission abilities. Accordingly, the size and emission characteristics of GQDs could be tuned by varying the hydrothermal temperature.
The authors are very grateful to Xiamen University of Technology for the financial support.
